attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a haematemesis resulting in unconscious collapse. He is known to be a heavy drinker with Child's C liver cirrhosis.

On examination, he is peripherally warm and well perfused, and does not appear pale or clammy. His blood pressure is 128/82 mmHg, pulse 126/min, temperature 36.9C, respiratory rate 23/min and SpO2 99% on room air. There is large ascites, but the abdomen is soft and not tense. On auscultation the chest is clear. There is no peripheral oedema.

He is given 2 L Hartmann's solution, terlipressin and started on a course of ceftriaxone. He stabilises, with no further episodes of bleeding, and is transferred to the ward following one day of monitoring in the intensive care unit.

His blood tests today show:

  • Haemoglobin 110g/L [115-155]

  • Platelets 140 x10^9/L [150-450]

  • White cell count 8.0 x10^9/L [4.0-10.0]

  • INR 1.4 [0.9-1.2]

  • aPTT 40 seconds [24-38]

Short term management will focus on endoscopic variceal eradication and encouraging abstinence. That will be followed by consideration of how best to reduce the longer term risks of further variceal haemorrhage.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following long term treatments is most appropriate?
attributes.explanation:
This man likely has oesophageal varices secondary to long-standing liver disease. The best ongoing management for this man's oesophageal varices is a non-selective beta blocker such as propranolol or carvedilol. There is increasing evidence in the literature that carvedilol can improve survival in patients with advanced chronic liver disease.

Endoscopic band ligation is often used in conjunction with beta blocker therapy in not actively bleeding varices. The blood tests are consistent with that of a man with Child's C liver cirrhosis and reduced synthetic liver function. The beta blocker will be prescribed on the understanding that the ascites is going to be treated. These agents will potentially reduce cardiac output and in the presence of refractory ascites in a patient with cirrhosis, there may be reduced renal perfusion and the risk of hepatorenal syndrome.

Loop diuretics (furosemide) and potassium-sparing diuretics (spironolactone) are used to help manage ascites associated with cirrhosis - not for oesophageal varices.

Somatostatin analogs (octreotide) can be used as an infusion to help control acute variceal haemorrhage by reducing portal hypertension, however limited mortality benefit has been shown.

TIPS can be attempted in repeated variceal bleeding after failed beta blocker therapy and/or endoscopic ligation, but would currently be inappropriate in this man.
learningPoints: To understand the pathophysiology behind the formation of oesophageal varices.
To understand the therapeutic options in treating oesophageal varices.
references: Kockerling D, Nathwani R, Forlano R, Manousou P, Mullish BH, Dhar A. Current and future pharmacological therapies for managing cirrhosis and its complications. World J Gastroenterol. 2019 Feb 28;25(8):888-908.

Gillespie S-L, Hanrahan TP, Rockey DC, et al. Review article: controversies surrounding the use of carvedilol and other beta blockers in the management of portal hypertension and cirrhosis. Aliment Pharmacol Ther. 2023; 57:454–63.
